facade_colour: white/cream painted brick; dark brown/maroon trim on gable dormers and bargeboard

hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: The property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, the property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The property retains many of its original building features including its cross-gable roofline, gable dormer windows, wooden barge board detailing and finials, first storey bay window, single-storey porch, and brick cladding. Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. The property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: One-way | Residential street | Tree-lined | Heritage character

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: white/cream painted brick; dark brown/maroon trim on gable dormers and bargeboard
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Well-maintained painted brick facade; generous front lawn and garden; single-storey covered porch; decorative bargeboard and finials
photo_observations.window_details: Multiple windows at ground floor including bay window under porch; upper floor has paired windows in gable dormers
photo_observations.door_details: Single front door accessed via covered porch; glass-panelled door
photo_observations.cornice_details: Decorative wooden bargeboard with ornate scrollwork at gable peaks; maroon/dark brown painted
photo_observations.bay_window_details: Ground floor bay window visible under porch overhang
photo_observations.porch_details: Single-storey open covered porch with arched opening; white painted posts and railing; lattice below; 4-5 steps up
photo_observations.chimney_details: One chimney visible at left rear, brick
photo_observations.decorative_details: Ornate wooden bargeboard at both gable peaks; pointed finials at apexes; decorative lattice below porch
photo_observations.roof_type_observed: Cross-gable with steep pitch and decorative bargeboard
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Painted over original brick; but bargeboard, finials, porch, and gable form all appear original or well-restored
photo_observations.notes: Excellent match to HCD - retains cross-gable, gable dormers, bargeboard, finials, bay window, porch, brick cladding (painted)

notes: Excellent match to HCD - retains cross-gable, gable dormers, bargeboard, finials, bay window, porch, brick cladding (painted)

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its house-form, detached typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 3, 3 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double hung, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_expression: Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, original windows, bay window, cornice.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. The property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles.

facade_description: Row two-storey house-form in white/cream painted brick; dark brown/maroon trim on gable dormers and bargeboard with a gable roof. Features include a double-height projecting bay, decorative bargeboards and a decorative cornice. Painted over original brick; but bargeboard, finials, porch, and gable form all appear original or well-restored. Pre-1889 construction.
heritage_summary: Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. The property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles.
